topic: A person discovers their child has the same teacher they had
raw_output:
[story 1]

The glossy brochure for the new preschool had landed on the kitchen counter, a stark white rectangle mocking me. I traced the embossed lettering with a shaky finger. Then, the name swam into focus: Ms. Eleanor Vance. My breath hitched. It was impossible. Ms. Vance. The woman whose gentle voice had somehow soothed my own anxieties during those chaotic early years. Now, she was teaching my seven-year-old, Leo. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, pulling at my chest. I stood by the window, watching the neighbor's dog chase a ball, the sound of its happy bark grating against the sudden, sharp silence inside my head. Every perfectly organized shelf in the pantry seemed to tilt. I needed air, desperately.

[story 2]

I stared at the school registration form, the ink blurring beneath my gaze. Leo’s name was written down, and next to it, the familiar script: Ms. Vance. A wave of nausea washed through me, making my hands tremble so violently I had to grip the edge of the desk. I reread the line, then the signature. It felt like a physical blow. My jaw clenched so hard I could taste copper. I started pacing the length of the living room, my movements jerky and uncoordinated. Why? What did this mean? The world seemed to have suddenly sharpened into unbearable edges, every sound amplified, every thought a frantic, buzzing insect trapped behind my eyes. I sank onto the sofa, burying my face in my hands, the fabric of the cushion offering no real comfort against the rising tide of sheer disbelief.

[story 3]

The phone call had ended, leaving a ringing void in the silence of the hallway. I kept replaying the conversation—the casual mention of the teacher, the shared anecdote about a difficult project. Now, it was real. My child, Leo, was in Ms. Vance's class. The air in the room felt thick, heavy, suffocating. I walked into the spare room, turning around repeatedly, as if expecting the reality to dissolve. My palms were slick with sweat. I looked at the framed photo of Leo from last summer, his bright smile suddenly seeming distant, unreal. I walked back to the door, my steps slow and deliberate, each one an effort against a mounting pressure behind my sternum. I just needed to sit down, to stop moving, to let the sheer weight of the situation settle, but my limbs felt too restless, too keyed up to comply.
